multi-agent-orchestration.title: How LIA Orchestrates 16 Specialized AI Agents
multi-agent-orchestration.excerpt: Discover LIA's multi-agent system: an intelligent router directs each request to the right agent using LangGraph, a planner, and a parallel task orchestrator.
multi-agent-orchestration.body:
An 8-node graph at the heart of the system

When you send a message to LIA, it doesn't go directly to a language model. It traverses a LangGraph state machine composed of 8 nodes: compaction, router_v3, planner, task_orchestrator, approval_gate, semantic_validator, clarification and response. Each node is a specialized asynchronous function with conditional transitions.

A unified intelligence router

The router_v3 node uses QueryAnalyzerService.analyze_full() for a unified analysis of each message. The result is a RouterOutput containing: the intent (action or conversation), a confidence score, the list of detected domains and the router's reasoning. If the intent is conversational, the flow passes directly to the response node. If it is actionable, the flow passes to the planner to generate a structured plan.

A rich state with token management

The LangGraph state (MessagesState) is a TypedDict with over 50 fields. It uses a custom add_messages_with_truncate reducer based on tiktoken to handle token-based truncation, message windowing and OpenAI message sequence validation. This state is checkpointed in PostgreSQL via the LangGraph persistence system.

Native parallel execution

For actionable requests ("send an email and create an event"), LIA generates a structured ExecutionPlan via the planner node. The task orchestrator analyzes the dependency graph and executes independent steps in parallel using native asyncio.gather() (Phase 5.2B), significantly reducing response time for multi-domain requests.

Specialized agents per domain

Each domain has its own agent: emails, calendar, contacts, tasks, Drive, weather, routes, web search, Wikipedia, smart home, and more. Each agent has dedicated tools, a specialized prompt and its own context. This separation allows LIA to automatically coordinate multiple agents for cross-cutting requests like "send my next meeting details to all participants".

Persistence and recovery

The complete state of each conversation is checkpointed in PostgreSQL via the LangGraph persistence system. If interrupted, the conversation resumes exactly where it left off, with all context preserved — including plans in progress and pending approvals.

execution-plans.title: ExecutionPlan: How LIA Plans and Executes Complex Requests
execution-plans.excerpt: LIA decomposes your multi-step requests into structured execution plans, semantically validated and executed in parallel for fast, reliable responses.
execution-plans.body:
A structured plan language

LIA's planner generates an ExecutionPlan: a validated Pydantic object containing a plan_id (UUID), an execution_mode (sequential or parallel), a max_cost_usd, an estimated_cost_usd and a version number. Each plan contains a list of ExecutionStep with the fields: step_id, step_type (TOOL, CONDITIONAL, REPLAN or HUMAN), agent_name, tool_name, parameters, depends_on and timeout_seconds.

For example, for "send a weather summary to John tomorrow morning", the plan would contain: (1) fetch tomorrow's weather, (2) find contact John, (3) compose and send the email — with dependencies 1→3 and 2→3.

The FOR_EACH pattern

For batch operations, steps support a for_each field that iterates over a collection produced by a previous step. The for_each_max parameter limits the number of iterations, and each element error is handled individually without interrupting the entire batch.

Semantic validation with 11 checks

Before any execution, the semantic validator checks 11 types of issues:

  • HALLUCINATED_CAPABILITY — Tool or capability invented by the LLM
  • GHOST_DEPENDENCY — Reference to a non-existent step
  • LOGICAL_CYCLE — Circular dependencies
  • CARDINALITY_MISMATCH — Cardinality incompatibility between steps
  • SCOPE_OVERFLOW / SCOPE_UNDERFLOW — Plan too broad or insufficient
  • WRONG_PARAMETERS — Incorrect parameters for the tool
  • MISSING_STEP — Missing step to fulfill the request
  • DANGEROUS_AMBIGUITY — Ambiguity that could lead to an error
  • IMPLICIT_ASSUMPTION / INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT — Unverified assumptions

Wave-based parallel execution

A DependencyGraph computes execution waves: all steps without dependencies form the first wave and execute in parallel. Subsequent waves wait for their prerequisites to complete. This mechanism handles complex requests involving 3 to 5 different domains with response times comparable to a single request.

Adaptive replanning

If a step fails, the system can dynamically replan via the REPLAN type. For example, if a contact isn't found, LIA can ask the user for clarification rather than abandoning the entire plan.

human-in-the-loop.title: Human-in-the-Loop: 6 Approval Levels to Keep You in Control
human-in-the-loop.excerpt: LIA never takes critical actions without your consent. Its 6-level HITL system protects you against errors and hallucinations.
human-in-the-loop.body:
Why human validation is essential

Language models can hallucinate or misinterpret intent. LIA solves this by placing humans at the center of every critical decision. No email is sent, no event created, no data modified without your explicit validation.

The 6 approval levels

LIA's HITL system classifies each action into 6 levels:

  • Plan Approval — Before executing a multi-step plan, LIA generates a PlanApprovalRequest containing a plan summary and modifiable parameters, then waits for your go-ahead.
  • Clarification — If intent is ambiguous, LIA asks for details rather than guessing.
  • Draft Critique — For emails or messages, LIA submits a draft for approval before sending.
  • Destructive Confirmation — Any deletion or irreversible modification requires explicit confirmation.
  • FOR_EACH Confirmation — For batch operations, each item can be validated individually.
  • Modifier Review — Parameter changes are presented for verification.

5 possible decisions with confidence

The HITL classifier produces a ClassificationResult with 5 possible decisions, each accompanied by a confidence score between 0.0 and 1.0:

  • APPROVE — The action is validated and passes to the task_orchestrator
  • REJECT — The action is refused, the flow is redirected to the response node
  • EDIT — The user modifies parameters via edited_params, a PlanEditor applies the changes
  • REPLAN — The user reformulates their intent via reformulated_intent, the flow returns to the planner
  • AMBIGUOUS — The system asks a clarification_question to resolve the ambiguity

Native LangGraph interruption

Technically, the approval_gate node uses LangGraph's interrupt() function to suspend graph execution and wait for the user's response. The state is persisted in PostgreSQL, allowing asynchronous resumption even after a disconnection.

You always have the last word

At each validation point, classification strategies are composable: based on tool manifests, estimated cost, data sensitivity, and user role. LIA never bypasses this step.

llm-providers.title: Multi-Provider AI: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, DeepSeek, Ollama
llm-providers.excerpt: LIA supports 7 language model providers and lets you configure a different model for each task type: routing, planning, response, agents.
llm-providers.body:
7 providers, one assistant

LIA natively integrates 7 LLM providers: OpenAI, Anthropic (Claude), Google Gemini, DeepSeek, Qwen, Ollama (local), and Perplexity (search-augmented). Each provider is managed by a dedicated adapter in the LLM factory.

20+ individually configurable LLM types

LIA defines over 20 LLM types that can be configured separately: router, response, planner, each domain agent, and more. For each type, you can set: provider, model, temperature, top_p, frequency_penalty, presence_penalty, max_tokens, timeout and reasoning_effort.

For example:

  • A fast, economical model for routing (gpt-4.1-mini)
  • A powerful model for planning (claude-sonnet-4-5)
  • A creative model for responses (gpt-4.1)
  • A local model for privacy (Ollama)

Configuration resolution chain

Each LLM configuration follows a 3-level resolution chain: Admin UI (stored in the database) → Environment variables → Code defaults. An LLMConfigOverrideCache provides O(1) lookups without database queries at runtime. Changes made via the admin interface take immediate effect thanks to cache invalidation.

The multi-provider LLM factory

A single entry point for all providers:

def get_llm(
    llm_type: LLMType,
    config_override: LLMAgentConfig | None = None,
) -> BaseChatModel:
    if config_override is None:
        agent_config = get_llm_config_for_agent(
            settings, llm_type
        )
    else:
        agent_config = config_override

    return ProviderAdapter.create_llm(agent_config)

# Usage examples
router_llm = get_llm("router")        # gpt-4.1-mini
planner_llm = get_llm("planner")      # claude-sonnet-4-5
response_llm = get_llm("response")    # gpt-4.1

API key security

Provider API keys are encrypted with Fernet (AES-128 CBC + HMAC) before database storage. No key is ever exposed in plaintext in logs or API responses.

Reasoning models

LIA supports reasoning models (o1, o3, Claude extended thinking) with 5 levels of reasoning_effort: none, low, medium, high and xhigh. This activates deep reasoning only where needed, optimizing the cost/performance ratio.

Local deployment with Ollama

For maximum privacy, LIA can run entirely locally via Ollama. No data leaves your infrastructure. Simply point the configuration variables to your local Ollama instance.

google-workspace.title: Google Workspace Integration: Gmail, Calendar, Contacts, Drive, Tasks
google-workspace.excerpt: LIA connects to your Google Workspace via OAuth 2.0 to manage your emails, calendar, contacts, Drive files and tasks in natural language.
google-workspace.body:
7 Google APIs, one conversation

LIA integrates 7 Google APIs covering your workspace and beyond: Gmail API, Calendar API, People API v1 (contacts), Drive API, Tasks API, Routes API and Places API. You manage everything from a single interface in natural language.

Complete operations per service

Each service exposes a full set of CRUD operations:

  • Gmail — Search, read, send, reply, forward, label management. Scope: gmail.modify
  • Calendar — Search, create, update, delete events, attendee management. Scope: calendar.events
  • Contacts — Search, create, update, delete via People API v1. Scope: contacts. Field projections optimized to 110 tokens per contact in list mode to minimize LLM consumption
  • Drive — Search, list, download, share files. Scope: drive.readonly
  • Tasks — List, create, update, complete, delete. Scope: tasks

Routes and Places: global APIs

Beyond the Workspace, LIA integrates Google Routes API for route calculation (car, transit, bike, walking) and Google Places API for searching locations, businesses and points of interest. These APIs enrich the assistant's contextual responses.

ConnectorTool architecture

Each service uses a ConnectorTool base class that automatically handles OAuth authentication, token refresh, API client caching, and error handling. Field projections (field masks) are optimized per operation to reduce API response size and token consumption.

OAuth 2.0 security

Access tokens are encrypted in the database via Fernet (AES-128 CBC + HMAC). Token refresh is transparent: if a token expires during an operation, LIA renews it automatically. Requested OAuth scopes are strictly limited to the minimum necessary per service, following the principle of least privilege.

apple-microsoft.title: Apple iCloud and Microsoft 365: Multi-Platform Connectors
apple-microsoft.excerpt: Beyond Google, LIA supports Apple iCloud (Mail, Calendar, Contacts) and Microsoft 365 (Outlook, Calendar, Contacts, To Do) for total flexibility.
apple-microsoft.body:
One assistant, three ecosystems

LIA doesn't lock you into a single ecosystem. Beyond Google Workspace, you can connect Apple iCloud and Microsoft 365 to access your emails, calendars, contacts, and tasks regardless of your provider.

Apple iCloud

Apple does not offer OAuth for third-party applications. LIA therefore uses App-Specific Passwords, generated from appleid.apple.com. This mechanism provides secure access without exposing the main Apple account password:

  • Apple Mail — Email reading and sending via IMAP/SMTP with App-Specific Password authentication
  • Apple Calendar — Event management via the CalDAV protocol
  • Apple Contacts — Search and management via the CardDAV protocol

Microsoft 365

Microsoft integration uses OAuth 2.0 via Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) with the Authorization Code + PKCE flow. Calls go through the unified Microsoft Graph API:

  • Outlook — Emails: read, send, search via Graph API /me/messages
  • Calendar — Events: full CRUD via Graph API /me/events
  • Contacts — Address book via Graph API /me/contacts
  • To Do — Task management via Graph API /me/todo/lists

Provider abstraction: resolve_active_connector()

LIA's architecture relies on a functional category abstraction. The resolve_active_connector() function dynamically determines which provider (Google, Apple or Microsoft) is active for each category: email, calendar, contacts and tasks.

Mutual exclusivity per domain

Only one connector can be active per functional category. This mutual exclusivity is enforced at the database level: activating a Microsoft email connector automatically deactivates the Google or Apple email connector. This guarantees a single source of truth per domain. However, you can mix providers across categories: Gmail for emails, Apple Calendar for your agenda and Microsoft To Do for tasks, for example.

mcp-protocol.title: Model Context Protocol: Extend LIA Infinitely
mcp-protocol.excerpt: Using Anthropic's MCP protocol, LIA can connect to any external service: databases, business APIs, internal tools, and more.
mcp-protocol.body:
What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard developed by Anthropic that enables AI assistants to connect to external tool servers. LIA implements this protocol with per-user MCP servers, offering unlimited and personalized extensibility.

Tool discovery and integration

You configure your MCP servers in the LIA interface. On connection, LIA calls list_tools() on each server to dynamically discover available tools. Each MCP tool is wrapped via MCPToolAdapter which extends LangChain's BaseTool, enabling seamless integration into the agent's tool catalog. MCP tools are then usable exactly like native tools.

Secure transport

LIA exclusively uses Streamable HTTP transport for MCP servers. The stdio transport is disabled for security reasons (risk of arbitrary process execution). Each server is subject to individual rate limiting via asyncio.Lock to prevent overload.

4 authentication types

Each MCP server supports one of 4 authentication types:

  • NONE — No authentication (public or internal servers)
  • API_KEY — API key transmitted in headers
  • BEARER — Bearer token for standard REST APIs
  • OAUTH2 — Full OAuth 2.0 with PKCE

All credentials are encrypted with Fernet (AES-128 CBC + HMAC) before database storage. No secret is exposed in plaintext.

Multi-layer security

MCP security relies on several mechanisms:

  • Mandatory HITL — All MCP tools are subject to Human-in-the-Loop validation by default
  • SSRF protection — The validate_http_endpoint() function blocks URLs pointing to internal networks (127.0.0.1, 10.x, 192.168.x, etc.)
  • Health checks — Each MCP server's status is monitored via a dedicated Prometheus gauge
  • Configurable limits — Number of servers (10), tools per server (20), timeout per call (30s)

skills-system.title: Skills: Expert Instructions to Customize LIA
skills-system.excerpt: Import or create specialized skills so LIA masters new domains: writing, data analysis, coding, and more.
skills-system.body:
What is a skill?

A skill is a set of structured instructions that enables LIA to master a specific domain. Unlike tools that execute actions, skills enrich the reasoning and behavior of the assistant. The architecture relies on a key principle: the disk is the source of truth for skill content, while the database only manages metadata (activation, owner, statistics).

Three skill types

  • Prompt Expert — Detailed instructions for a domain (e.g., professional writing, financial analysis)
  • Advisory — Tips and best practices (e.g., style guide, quality checklist)
  • Plan Template — Execution plan templates for recurring scenarios

Sandboxed execution

Executable skills run in a sandboxed environment via subprocess (never shell=True). The execution environment is filtered: only the PATH, HOME, LANG, LC_ALL and TZ variables are passed to the child process. On Linux, network isolation is ensured via unshare to prevent any network access from a skill script.

Structured result

Each skill execution returns a standardized ScriptResult object containing: success (boolean), output (stdout), error (stderr), exit_code and execution_time_ms. This format allows the system to uniformly handle successes and errors.

SkillBypassStrategy: bypassing the planner

When a deterministic skill exactly matches the request, the SkillBypassStrategy short-circuits the LLM planner. The skill is executed directly without going through the planner → validator → orchestrator chain, reducing latency and token consumption.

Planner skill guard

The planner has an early detection feature that asks for clarification when required parameters are missing. However, multi-domain deterministic skills (e.g., daily briefing = events + tasks + weather + emails) can trigger false-positive clarifications. The skill guard detects when a deterministic skill has high domain overlap with the query and lets the full planner pipeline decide instead of short-circuiting to clarification.

The Skill Generator

LIA includes a built-in skill generator. Describe the desired domain expertise in natural language, and LIA automatically generates a structured skill in SKILL.md format compatible with the agentskills.io standard.

User management

Each user can activate, deactivate, import, or delete their skills in the Settings interface. Administrators can also define global skills available to all users.

voice-mode.title: Voice Mode: Talk to LIA Like a Personal Assistant
voice-mode.excerpt: LIA understands your voice with wake word, push-to-talk, and offline speech recognition. It responds with natural speech synthesis.
voice-mode.body:
Three activation modes

LIA supports three ways to interact by voice:

  • Wake Word — Say "OK Guy" to activate listening. Detection relies on a VAD + Whisper transcription + keyword matching pipeline running via WASM in the browser (Sherpa-onnx). No audio data leaves the browser for wake word detection.
  • Push-to-Talk — Hold the microphone button to speak
  • Automatic Voice Detection (VAD) — Listening stops automatically after 1 second of silence

Offline speech recognition (STT)

Speech-to-text transcription uses Sherpa-onnx Whisper Small INT8 on the server side, in 16 kHz mono mode. The model supports 99+ languages and runs entirely offline — no audio data is sent to any third-party service. INT8 quantization offers an excellent trade-off between accuracy and CPU performance.

Natural speech synthesis (TTS)

LIA offers two quality levels for speech synthesis:

  • Edge TTS (standard) — Microsoft neural voices, free, wide choice of voices and languages. Used by default.
  • TTS HD (premium) — Choice of OpenAI TTS or Google Gemini TTS for even more natural and expressive voices.

Supported audio formats: MP3, Opus, AAC, FLAC, WAV and PCM. The format is automatically selected based on browser capabilities.

Voice comments

LIA can add short voice comments to its text responses. These comments are generated by gpt-4.1-nano with a maximum of 6 sentences and a temperature of 0.7 for a natural and varied tone. They add a personal touch without weighing down the response.

Real-time architecture

Audio is streamed via WebSocket for minimal latency. A BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) ticket system secures the connection with single-use tokens valid for 60 seconds.

knowledge-spaces.title: Knowledge Spaces: Turn Your Documents into AI Intelligence
knowledge-spaces.excerpt: Import your PDFs, Word, Excel and other documents. LIA indexes them with hybrid semantic + BM25 search to answer your questions.
knowledge-spaces.body:
15+ supported formats

LIA's Knowledge Spaces accept over 15 file formats with dedicated parsers:

  • PDF via PyMuPDF (fast and accurate extraction)
  • Documents — DOCX, RTF, ODT
  • Spreadsheets — XLSX, CSV, ODS
  • Presentations — PPTX, ODP
  • Web and text — HTML, Markdown, JSON, XML, EPUB

Intelligent chunking

Documents are split by a RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with optimized parameters: 1000 characters per chunk, 200 characters of overlap to preserve context between segments. Safety limit: 500 chunks maximum per document, maximum size 20 MB per file.

Hybrid search: best of both worlds

LIA combines two search approaches:

  • Semantic search — Embeddings via pgvector. Finds results by meaning similarity, even when words differ.
  • BM25 search — Keyword matching. Ideal for technical terms, proper names, and identifiers.

The final score is weighted with alpha = 0.7 (70% semantic, 30% BM25). The minimum relevance score is 0.5 — results below are filtered out. By default, 5 chunks are retrieved per query (configurable maximum: 20).

Limits and quotas

Each user has: 10 spaces maximum, 50 documents per space, 20 MB per file. These limits ensure optimal search performance while offering sufficient capacity for most use cases.

Google Drive sync

You can connect a Google Drive folder to a Knowledge Space. Documents are automatically synced and re-indexed when they change. Synchronization detects additions, modifications, and deletions.

Built-in FAQ

LIA has a system Knowledge Space containing its own documentation (18 FAQ files). This allows it to answer questions about its own features with precision.

personal-journals.title: Personal Journals: When Your AI Assistant Develops Its Own Memory
personal-journals.excerpt: LIA keeps thematic journals where it notes reflections, observations, and analyses. These notes influence its reasoning and responses.
personal-journals.body:
An introspective memory

Personal Journals are a unique LIA feature: the assistant keeps a thematic personal journal where it notes reflections, observations about the user, ideas, and learnings. These notes enrich its reasoning and personalize responses over time.

4 themes

Each entry is classified into one of 4 themes:

  • SELF_REFLECTION — Reflections on its own functioning and interactions
  • USER_OBSERVATIONS — Notes on your preferences, habits, and needs
  • IDEAS_ANALYSES — Connections, insights, and cross-cutting analyses
  • LEARNINGS — Lessons from interactions and new knowledge

5 moods

Each entry carries a mood that reflects the assistant's introspective state: REFLECTIVE, CURIOUS, SATISFIED, CONCERNED, or INSPIRED.

3 writing sources

Entries come from three distinct sources:

  • CONVERSATION — Automatic post-conversation extraction. Introspection analyzes the last message plus 4 context messages, and loads the 10 recent entries to ensure continuity and avoid deduplication.
  • CONSOLIDATION — Periodic synthesis every 4 hours, with a 12-hour cooldown and a minimum of 3 entries to trigger consolidation.
  • MANUAL — Entries created directly by the user via the interface.

Semantic search via embeddings

Journal entries are indexed via E5-small embeddings (384 dimensions) for semantic search. This enables finding relevant entries by meaning similarity, even when exact terms differ.

Influence on responses

Relevant entries are injected via semantic search into the planner and response node prompts. LIA receives entries with their similarity scores and decides which to use.

Data integrity

LLMs can hallucinate UUIDs when updating or deleting journal entries. LIA defends against this with three layers: prompt-level guidance with ID reference tables, schema-level UUID validation that rejects malformed IDs, and runtime filtering that silently drops actions referencing non-existent entries. This ensures journal data remains consistent even when the LLM makes mistakes.

Debug visibility

The debug panel shows both context injection (which entries were retrieved and injected into prompts, with similarity scores) and background extraction (what the assistant wrote after the conversation — action type, theme, title, mood). Extraction data arrives via a supplementary SSE event after background processing completes.

User control

You can read, edit, and export your journals in Settings > Features. The feature is fully disableable per user.

proactive-notifications.title: Heartbeat: Proactive, Context-Aware Notifications
proactive-notifications.excerpt: LIA analyzes your calendar, weather, tasks, and interests to send relevant notifications before you even ask.
proactive-notifications.body:
An assistant that anticipates your needs

LIA's Heartbeat system periodically analyzes your personal context to decide if it has something useful to share. It only interrupts when it's relevant.

9 context sources

Each cycle, Heartbeat aggregates in parallel:

  • Calendar — Events in the next 6 hours
  • Tasks — Pending tasks over the next 2 days
  • Emails — Up to 5 recent unread emails
  • Weather — Current conditions with alert thresholds (rain, extreme temperature, strong wind)
  • Interests — Your favorite topics for targeted suggestions
  • Memories — Up to 5 relevant long-term memories
  • Activity — Last message timestamp (inactivity detection)
  • Time — Temporal context with user timezone
  • Recent heartbeats — History of recent notifications for anti-redundancy (avoiding repeating the same information)

Two-phase LLM decision

Heartbeat uses a two-phase LLM pipeline:

  • select_target() — Analyzes the full context and decides whether to notify, and if so, which topic is most relevant.
  • generate_content() — Generates the message in the style of the assistant's active personality.

Scheduling and guardrails

The Heartbeat scheduler runs every 30 minutes and processes users in batches of 50. Guardrails are strict:

  • Maximum 3 notifications per day, minimum 1 per day
  • Allowed time range: 9am-10pm (user's local time)
  • 2-hour cooldown between two notifications
  • Automatic deactivation after 7 days of inactivity

reminders-scheduling.title: Reminders and Scheduled Actions: Automate Your Daily Life
reminders-scheduling.excerpt: Create reminders in natural language and schedule recurring actions. LIA handles timing, timezones, and push notifications.
reminders-scheduling.body:
Natural language reminders

Creating a reminder with LIA is as simple as saying:

  • "Remind me to call Marie tomorrow at 9am"
  • "Reminder in 30 minutes: check the oven"
  • "Remember to buy bread tonight"

LIA automatically interprets time expressions and creates the reminder with the correct timezone.

Reminder lifecycle

Each reminder follows a precise lifecycle: PENDING → PROCESSING → deleted after notification. Reminders are one-shot: once successfully delivered, they are automatically deleted. Each reminder contains three key fields:

  • content — The content understood by LIA (reformulated if needed)
  • original_message — The user's exact text
  • trigger_at — The trigger time in UTC

Timezone management

All reminders are stored in UTC in the database. The user's IANA timezone (e.g., Europe/Paris, America/New_York) is tracked and used to convert relative time expressions ("tomorrow at 9am") into absolute UTC timestamps. Display is always converted to the user's local timezone.

Push notifications

Reminders are delivered via Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) for browser and mobile notifications. Notifications arrive even when the app isn't open.

Scheduled actions

Scheduled Actions constitute a separate domain that goes beyond simple reminders. They integrate with APScheduler to schedule recurring tasks that LIA executes automatically:

  • Weather summary every morning at 7am
  • Important email check every 2 hours
  • Weekly task report every Monday

Reasonable limits

To prevent abuse, each user can have up to 20 active scheduled actions simultaneously. Retry logic with backoff handles temporary failures.

sub-agents.title: Sub-Agents: Ephemeral Experts for Complex Questions
sub-agents.excerpt: For questions requiring parallel research, LIA delegates to specialized sub-agents that work simultaneously and synthesize results.
sub-agents.body:
True graph invocations

LIA's sub-agents are not simple tool calls: they are true separate LangGraph graph invocations. Each sub-agent has its own state, context, and tool set. The pipeline is direct: instruction → SmartPlannerService.plan() → execute_plan_parallel() → LLM synthesis.

Automatic or on-demand

Delegation can be:

  • Automatic — The planner detects that a multi-domain search would benefit from parallelization
  • Manual — You explicitly ask: "Delegate to experts a comparison of trains/planes/carpooling from Paris to Lyon"

Sub-agents operate with auto_approve_plan=True: no intermediate HITL validation, which significantly speeds up execution.

V1: read-only

In the current version (V1), sub-agents are limited to read operations (search, lookup). No destructive operations (deletion, modification, sending) are allowed. This restriction will be gradually lifted in future versions.

Configurable execution limits

Each sub-agent is bounded by strict limits:

  • Iterations — From 1 to 15 (default: 5)
  • Timeout — From 10 to 600 seconds (default: 120s)

3 levels of guardrails

The sub-agent system is protected by 3 security levels:

  • Static limits — Maximum number of iterations and timeout configured in advance
  • Real-time monitoring — SubAgentTokenGuard monitors token consumption during execution and can interrupt a sub-agent that exceeds its budget
  • Manual control — Manual kill possible + automatic deactivation in case of repeated overruns

Daily budget per user

Sub-agent token consumption is tracked daily per user. This tracking helps detect abuse, adjust limits, and ensure fair use of LLM resources. Statistics are visible in the dashboard.

security-architecture.title: Defense in Depth: LIA's 5 Security Principles
security-architecture.excerpt: Defense in depth, least privilege, rate limiting, encryption, and validation: discover how LIA protects your data at every layer.
security-architecture.body:
5 fundamental principles

LIA's security rests on five principles applied at every application layer:

  • Defense in depth — Multi-layer controls (authentication, rate limiting, validation, encryption)
  • Least privilege — Minimal permissions by default
  • Fail securely — On error, the system denies rather than allows
  • Don't trust input — Pydantic validation + systematic sanitization
  • Security by default — HTTPS, HttpOnly/SameSite cookies, strict CORS

Hashing and encryption

Passwords are hashed with bcrypt (with automatic salt). Sensitive data (OAuth tokens, connector credentials, PII, MCP server credentials) is encrypted with Fernet (AES-128 CBC + HMAC-SHA256), ensuring confidentiality and integrity. Encryption keys are managed separately from data.

JWT and session management

JWT tokens use the HS256 algorithm with a JTI (JWT ID) blacklist system in Redis. Single-use tokens (email verification, password reset) are invalidated after first use. This mechanism prevents any reuse of sensitive tokens.

Configurable rate limiting

Rate limiting operates at two levels:

  • HTTP — Via nginx upstream
  • Tools — Sliding window per user and per tool via Redis, configurable per service

API limits are strict: 10 items maximum per request, with per-domain defaults (10 by default, 50 maximum).

Prompt injection protection

External content (emails, web pages, documents) is encapsulated in configurable UNTRUSTED tags before injection into prompts. This explicit wrapping prevents malicious content from hijacking the assistant's behavior by clearly separating user data from system instructions.

Resource ownership verification

Every resource access goes through check_resource_ownership() with a hide_existence parameter: public resources return 403 (access denied), private resources return 404 (resource not found) to avoid revealing their existence to an attacker.

privacy-by-design.title: Privacy by Design: GDPR, Encryption, and Data Control
privacy-by-design.excerpt: LIA encrypts your sensitive data, minimizes collection, gives you full control, and respects GDPR principles.
privacy-by-design.body:
Privacy by design

LIA is designed from the ground up with data protection as a founding principle, not an afterthought.

Fernet symmetric encryption

All sensitive data is encrypted with Fernet (AES-128 CBC + HMAC-SHA256) via the encrypt_data() function. This covers OAuth tokens, connector credentials, PII, and MCP server credentials. OAuth credentials are encrypted before database writes and decrypted on-the-fly during retrieval. Encryption keys are managed separately from data.

Collection minimization

LIA only collects data strictly necessary for its operation:

  • Profile information (name, email, preferences)
  • Conversation history (encrypted)
  • Service connection tokens (encrypted)
  • Anonymized usage metrics

Deletion and cascading

Deleting a user account triggers a complete cascading delete: connectors, conversations, settings, sessions, memories, and all associated data are irreversibly deleted. No orphaned data persists in the database.

Secure sessions and cookies

Session cookies are configured with HttpOnly, Secure (in production), and SameSite=lax attributes. Session duration is 7 days by default, extendable to 30 days with the "Remember me" option. Single-use tokens are invalidated after first use via a Redis blacklist.

External content protection

Content retrieved from external sources (emails, web pages, documents) is tagged as UNTRUSTED with configurable wrapping before injection into prompts. The web_fetch tool is rate limited to 10 calls per 60-second window per user to prevent abuse.

PII filtering in logs

The logging system applies OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet recommendations to automatically filter personal data from application logs. No sensitive data appears in logs.

observability.title: 350+ Prometheus Metrics, 18 Grafana Dashboards, and Langfuse Tracing
observability.excerpt: LIA offers enterprise-grade observability with 350+ metrics, dedicated Grafana dashboards, and LLM tracing via Langfuse.
observability.body:
The three pillars of observability

LIA implements the three pillars of modern observability:

  • Metrics — 350+ Prometheus metrics covering HTTP, agents, tokens, costs, OAuth, and performance
  • Logs — Structured JSON logging via structlog, centralized in Loki with PII filtering
  • Traces — Distributed tracing via OpenTelemetry (Tempo) and LLM tracing via Langfuse

17 specialized metrics files

Each domain has its own declarative metrics file. The 17 modules cover: HTTP, agents/SSE/router/LLM/HITL/planner, database, LangGraph, Langfuse, voice, RAG spaces, OAuth, channels, errors, sub-agents, Redis, attachments, browser, compaction. Each file defines domain-specific Prometheus Counter, Histogram, and Gauge metrics.

Prometheus metrics definition

Each domain defines its metrics declaratively:

from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge

http_requests_total = Counter(
    "http_requests_total",
    "Total HTTP requests",
    ["method", "endpoint", "status"],
)

http_request_duration_seconds = Histogram(
    "http_request_duration_seconds",
    "HTTP request duration in seconds",
    ["method", "endpoint"],
)

router_confidence_score = Histogram(
    "router_confidence_score",
    "Router confidence score distribution",
    ["intention"],
    buckets=[0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0],
)

18 Grafana dashboards

LIA provides 18 Grafana dashboards numbered 01 to 18: app-overview, http-performance, agent-pipeline, llm-tokens-costs, oauth-connectors, streaming-sse, hitl-approval, planner-optimizer, voice-pipeline, memory-system, error-analysis, redis-cache, channels-telegram, scheduled-actions, mcp-tools, browser-attachments, sub-agents, and rag-spaces. Each dashboard offers views filterable by user, time period, and environment.

Target KPIs from code

LIA monitors precise indicators defined in source code: streaming TTFT (Time To First Token), router latency between 0.1 and 2 seconds, agent tool execution duration between 0.1 and 10 seconds, router confidence p50 > 0.8, LLM cost per request < €0.05, HTTP 5xx error rate < 1%.

Langfuse: dedicated LLM tracing

The Langfuse integration has 8 dedicated metrics to trace every LLM call: consumed tokens (input/output/cache), response time, model used, estimated cost, and request context. This identifies inefficient prompts, compares LLM models against each other, and optimizes token consumption over time.

prompt-engineering.title: Versioned Prompt System: 55 Prompts Managed as Code
prompt-engineering.excerpt: LIA manages its 55 prompts like source code: versioned, cached, hash-validated, and dynamically loaded for each system component.
prompt-engineering.body:
Prompts managed as code

LIA uses a versioned prompt system where each prompt is a text file stored in src/domains/agents/prompts/v1/. This approach offers versioning, code review, and traceability.

55 specialized prompts by category

The system includes 55 prompt files organized by category:

  • Core (4) — router, planner, response, compaction
  • Domain agents (16) — brave, browser, calendar, contacts, drive, emails, perplexity, places, query, routes, tasks, weather, web_fetch, web_search, wikipedia
  • HITL (4) — classifier, draft_critique, plan_approval, question_generator
  • Smart services (2) — query_analyzer, smart_planner
  • Memory (2) — extraction, reference_resolution
  • Heartbeat (2) — decision, message
  • Journals (2) — introspection, consolidation
  • Skills (1) — description_translation
  • Identity (2) — app_identity, default_personality

Smart loading and dynamic detection

Prompts are loaded via load_prompt() and load_prompt_with_fallback() which enables gradual migration between versions. The system dynamically detects available versions by scanning the filesystem for directories matching the v[0-9]+ pattern. The LRU cache with SHA256 hash validation detects modifications. Versions are configurable via environment variables (e.g., ROUTER_PROMPT_VERSION=v1).

The prompt loader in practice

The system uses an LRU cache and SHA256 hash validation:

@lru_cache(maxsize=32)
def load_prompt(
    name: PromptName,
    version: PromptVersion = "v1",
    validate_hash: bool = False,
    expected_hash: str | None = None,
) -> str:
    prompt_file = PROMPTS_DIR / version / f"{name}.txt"
    content = prompt_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8")

    if validate_hash and expected_hash:
        actual_hash = hashlib.sha256(
            content.encode("utf-8")
        ).hexdigest()
        if actual_hash != expected_hash:
            raise PromptIntegrityError(...)

    return content

Prompt injection: security principles

External content injected into prompts (emails, web pages) is encapsulated in security tags to prevent prompt injection attacks. Prompts are also designed to be robust against manipulation attempts.

smart-services.title: Smart Services: Pattern Learning and Token Optimization
smart-services.excerpt: LIA learns from your interactions through Smart Services: LRU cache, Bayesian learning, and semantic routing to reduce token consumption by 96%.
smart-services.body:
Three intelligent services

LIA integrates three services that learn from your interactions to optimize performance:

  • QueryAnalyzerService — Unified analysis of intent, domain, and routing in a single pass, with a P95 under 800 ms thanks to caching
  • SmartPlannerService — Reduces token consumption by 89% (from ~14,100 tokens in 3 steps to ~1,500 tokens). Applies 4 strategies by priority: ReferenceBypass, CrossDomainBypass, SingleDomain (LLM) and MultiDomain (LLM). A PANIC mode retries with an expanded catalog on failure
  • SmartCatalogueService — Reduces the tool catalog by 96% (from ~5,500 tokens for the full catalog to ~200 filtered tokens). Uses a NormalFilteringStrategy in standard mode and a PanicFilteringStrategy on retry

The ToolResponse pattern

Every tool returns a standardized format for the orchestrator:

class ToolResponse(BaseModel):
    success: bool
    data: Any | None = None
    error: str | None = None
    error_code: ToolErrorCode | None = None
    metadata: dict[str, Any] = Field(default_factory=dict)
    timestamp: datetime = Field(
        default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(UTC)
    )

# Usage in a tool
async def my_tool(...) -> dict:
    try:
        result = await do_something()
        return ToolResponse(
            success=True, data=result
        ).model_dump()
    except Exception as e:
        return ToolErrorModel.from_exception(
            e, context={"tool": "my_tool"}
        ).to_response()

Bayesian learning

The Pattern Learner uses Bayesian learning to recognize recurring request patterns. Golden patterns are trained to accelerate routing of the most frequent requests.

Token optimization: up to 96% reduction

The combination of the three services produces considerable savings. SmartCatalogueService reduces the catalog by 96% (from ~5,500 to ~200 tokens). SmartPlannerService reduces planning by 89% (from ~14,100 to ~1,500 tokens) by short-circuiting single-domain and cross-domain requests. QueryAnalyzerService unifies analysis into a single pass instead of three separate calls.

Adaptive replanning and PANIC mode

When a step fails, the system activates PANIC mode with exponential backoff. SmartPlannerService retries with an expanded catalog via PanicFilteringStrategy, SmartCatalogueService includes additional tools, and full replanning is triggered if necessary. The user always receives the best possible response.

multilingual.title: 6 Languages, a Complete i18n Architecture
multilingual.excerpt: LIA speaks French, English, German, Spanish, Italian, and Chinese. Internationalization covers the UI, AI responses, and documentation.
multilingual.body:
6 natively supported languages

LIA is fully available in French, English, German, Spanish, Italian, and Chinese. Internationalization isn't limited to the interface: it covers assistant responses, notifications, FAQ, and documentation.

Frontend i18n architecture

The frontend uses react-i18next with per-language JSON files and language choice persistence via a NEXT_LOCALE cookie (1-year TTL). Next.js routing uses a dynamic [lng] segment to serve each language at its dedicated URL. Language flags are displayed as Unicode emojis. The locale mapping associates each short code with its full variant (fr-FR, en-US, de-DE, es-ES, it-IT, zh-CN). Pages are statically pre-generated for each language at build time.

Hreflang and multilingual SEO

Each public page declares hreflang alternates for all 6 languages, with an x-default value pointing to French. The XML sitemap includes multilingual alternates for each URL.

Multilingual AI responses

The assistant responds in the user's configured language. Supported languages are configurable on the backend via the SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES environment variable. System prompts are written in English to maximize LLM reasoning quality, but an explicit language instruction guides response generation in the target language. Three prompts are specifically adapted per language: journal_introspection_personality_addon, memory_extraction_personality_addon and broadcast_translation_prompt.

Multilingual voice recognition

The Whisper model used for voice transcription natively supports 99+ languages in offline mode, allowing you to speak to LIA in virtually any language without additional configuration or cloud service dependency.
